title: Engineers Week LinkedIn Post
draft:
It's Engineers Week, and we're celebrating the people behind the infrastructure we all count on. 🛠️

At SAME San Francisco Post, engineering isn't just a topic. It's the reason we exist. Our members design military installations, build civil works, manage federal construction projects, and mentor the students who'll do it next.

This year, we're looking forward to bringing the A/E/C community together with U.S. Coast Guard Civil Engineering Unit Oakland for an Industry Day on March 12. More details coming soon.

Whether you're a PE, a project manager, a surveyor, or a student still figuring out your path, this week is for you. 🎓

How did you get into engineering? Drop your story below. 👇

#SAMESF #EngineersWeek #EWeek2026 #MilitaryEngineers #AECCommunity #STEMeducation

*Registration link for the USCG Industry Day in first comment when available*
alt_version:
Every bridge, every base, every water system that works when you turn the tap. Engineers. 🛠️

This week is National Engineers Week, and at SAME San Francisco Post, we don't just observe it. We live it year-round.

Our Post sits at the intersection of military engineering and the private sector. We connect Coast Guard, Army Corps, Navy, and Air Force civil engineers with the A/E/C firms who build alongside them. We pair seasoned PEs with students just finding their path. We host the site visits, forums, and events where those conversations happen in person.

Here's what that looks like in 2026:

⛳ Funding STEM scholarships through our Golf Tournament and Holiday Gala
🏗️ Hosting site visits and technical forums connecting military and civilian engineers
⚓ This March, co-hosting an Industry Day with U.S. Coast Guard Civil Engineering Unit Oakland. Registration details dropping soon.

National Engineers Week started in 1951, around the same time our Post was getting its footing here in the Bay Area. George Washington, a surveyor and military engineer himself, would appreciate the connection.

To every engineer in our network: from the field offices at CEU Oakland to the design firms across the Bay, from the cadets to the professors at USF. This week is yours. 🇺🇸

How did you get into engineering? Tell us your story in the comments.

#SAMESF #EngineersWeek #EWeek2026 #MilitaryEngineers #AECCommunity #STEMeducation
notes: Alt Opening: "George Washington was a surveyor before he was a general. Engineering and national service have been connected from the very beginning."

title: TBT: Holiday Gala Photos Released
draft:
#ThrowbackThursday to a night that reminded us why this Post matters.

The Holiday Gala photos are in, and they tell the whole story. 📸

On December 11 at the Marines' Memorial Club, the room held service members, industry partners, USF engineering students, long-time members, and first-time guests. Each one part of the story that keeps SAME SF moving forward.

We heard from incoming president Scott MacCumbee, PE, who reflected on what gatherings like this really mean: returning to something simple and important. We watched leadership pass from Wendy Chen to Scott, a moment that connected our Post's past to its future.

And of course, the Marines' Memorial bulldogs kicked off the evening in style. 🐾

Every ticket, every sponsorship, every raffle entry that night went directly to STEM scholarships for University of San Francisco engineering and science students. That's the heart of the Gala.

📸 Browse the full photo album: https://web.photocircleapp.com/circles/Cuu8h9cXv4RQpzV258cXdN

See someone you know? Tag them below. 👇

#SAMESF #ThrowbackThursday #HolidayGala #AECCommunity #STEMeducation
alt_version:
The Holiday Gala is always more than a night out in the city. And now we have the photos to prove it. 📸

#ThrowbackThursday to December 11 at the Marines' Memorial Club, where our Post came together for an evening of connection, celebration, and purpose.

Looking through these photos brings it all back. The room was full, not just with people, but with the kind of energy that only happens when military, government, and A/E/C professionals share the same space with the students they're helping launch into careers.

A few moments that stand out:

🎤 Incoming president Scott MacCumbee, PE, reflecting on what it means to be human, and how gatherings like this bring us back to that.

🤝 The leadership transition from Wendy Chen to Scott, a bridge between everything our Post has built and everything still ahead.

🐾 The Marines' Memorial bulldogs, who showed up from 6:00 to 6:30 PM and set the tone for the whole evening, as they do every year.

🎓 STEM scholarships awarded to University of San Francisco engineering and science students, funded by every ticket and sponsorship in that room.

🎁 Toys for Tots collection supporting Marine Corps Reserve families during the holiday season.

This is what SAME SF looks like when we're at our best. Not a networking event, not a fundraiser. A community showing up for each other.

Thank you to every sponsor, volunteer, and guest who made this happen. And if you missed it? Start planning for December 2026. You won't want to miss the next one.

📸 Full photo album: https://web.photocircleapp.com/circles/Cuu8h9cXv4RQpzV258cXdN

Who do you spot in the gallery? Tag them. 👇

#SAMESF #ThrowbackThursday #HolidayGala #MarinesMemorialClub #AECCommunity #STEMeducation
notes: Alt Opening: "The best part of any SAME SF event isn't the program. It's the moment you look around the room and realize how many different uniforms, firms, and stories are sitting at the same table."

title: Call for Presentations — LinkedIn Post
draft:
Got a presentation sitting on your hard drive? We want to hear it.

SAME San Francisco Post is looking for members who have canned or pre-prepared presentations they'd be interested in sharing at one of our monthly in-person meetings.

Topics we're interested in:
- Engineering and construction project case studies
- Federal contracting lessons learned
- Technology and innovation in the A/E/C space
- Leadership and career development
- Military engineering or infrastructure topics

It doesn't have to be polished. It doesn't have to be new. If you've presented it at a conference, a lunch-and-learn, or an internal meeting and think our members would get value from it, we want to know.

Our monthly meetings are a chance to learn from each other. Help us fill the calendar.

Interested? Reach out to us at samesanfrancisco@gmail.com with your topic and a brief description.

#SAMESF #AECCommunity #MilitaryEngineers #ProfessionalDevelopment
alt_version:
We're building our 2026 meeting calendar, and we want your expertise on it.

SAME San Francisco Post hosts monthly in-person meetings where members connect, learn, and share. One of the best parts of these meetings? Hearing from our own members about the work they do, the projects they lead, and the lessons they've picked up along the way.

We're putting out a call: if you have a presentation, a talk, a case study, or a topic you'd be willing to share at an upcoming meeting, we'd love to hear from you.

What we're looking for:
- A/E/C project case studies (military, federal, or civilian)
- Federal contracting process, tips, or lessons learned
- New technology, tools, or methods in engineering and construction
- Leadership, mentorship, or career development topics
- STEM outreach and education approaches
- Military engineering or infrastructure projects

Format is flexible. It can be a 20-minute talk, a 45-minute deep dive, or a panel discussion. Slides, demos, or just a conversation. Whatever works for you and the topic.

Our members include military engineers, government project managers, A/E/C firm leaders, and students. The one thing they all have in common? They want to learn from people doing the work.

If you've given a talk at a conference, a company meeting, or a professional event and think it would fit, let us know.

Email samesanfrancisco@gmail.com with your topic and a brief description. We'll take it from there.

#SAMESF #AECCommunity #MilitaryEngineers #ProfessionalDevelopment #ServiceAndCommunity
notes: Alt Opening: "The best meetings aren't the ones where we sit and listen. They're the ones where our members share what they know. Your turn."

title: Seabee Birthday
draft:
84 years ago today, the U.S. Navy created a force that could build anything, anywhere, under any conditions.

Happy 84th Birthday to the Seabees.

Since 1942, the Naval Construction Battalions have built airfields under fire, repaired ports after storms, and constructed bases from the Pacific to the Middle East. "Can Do" isn't just a motto. It's a track record.

At SAME San Francisco Post, we work alongside the military engineering community every day. The Seabee spirit of building under pressure, solving problems in the field, and getting the job done is something every engineer and constructor in our network understands.

To the Seabees past and present: thank you for 84 years of building what the mission demands.

Can Do. 🇺🇸

#SAMESF #SeabeeBirthday #CanDo #MilitaryEngineers #NavalConstructionBattalion
alt_version:
"We Build, We Fight." For 84 years, that's exactly what the Seabees have done.

Happy Birthday to the U.S. Navy's Naval Construction Battalions, established March 5, 1942. Born out of necessity after Pearl Harbor, the Seabees were the answer to a simple question: who builds the infrastructure when the construction site is a war zone?

The answer? Construction workers, engineers, electricians, equipment operators, and tradespeople who raised their hands and shipped out. They built airfields in the Pacific in days, not months. They repaired bridges while taking fire. They turned remote coastlines into operational bases.

84 years later, that mission continues. Seabees deploy to build, repair, and sustain military installations and humanitarian projects around the world. They train alongside the communities they serve. And they carry forward a legacy that connects the construction industry to national defense in a way few other units can match.

At SAME San Francisco Post, that connection is personal. Our members include military engineers, civilian contractors, and A/E/C professionals who understand what it means to build something that matters. The Seabee ethos runs through everything we do.

To every Seabee, past and present: 84 years of "Can Do." Here's to many more. 🇺🇸

Know a Seabee? Tag them below and wish them a happy birthday.

#SAMESF #SeabeeBirthday #CanDo #MilitaryEngineers #NavalConstructionBattalion #WeBuiltWeFight
notes: Alt Opening: "Before there were Seabees, the military didn't have dedicated builders. After Pearl Harbor, that changed overnight. March 5, 1942."

title: USCG CEU Oakland Industry Day — LinkedIn Sequence
draft:
The U.S. Coast Guard is opening its doors to the A/E/C community. And SAME SF is co-hosting. ⚓

On March 12, Civil Engineering Unit Oakland is hosting an Industry Day for contractors, engineers, and firms interested in working with the Coast Guard.

Here's what's on the agenda:

🏗️ Briefing on CEUO's structure, branch chiefs, and contracting process
💬 Panel on opportunities, challenges, and improving the contracting process
🤝 Industry showcase and networking (Conference Room H)
📋 Field-specific breakout sessions (Conference Rooms A-E)
🔒 1-on-1 meetings with USCG contracting personnel (advance signup)

📍 CEU Oakland
📅 Thursday, March 12 | 2:00 - 5:00 PM
🎟️ Free. No cost to attend.
🍻 No-host social to follow

Whether you're a large firm or a small business exploring federal contracting for the first time, this is your chance to get in the room.

Registration details coming soon. Save the date. 📌

Questions? Reach out to LT Duncan Clark at Duncan.G.Clark@uscg.mil

#SAMESF #AECCommunity #MilitaryEngineers #USCG #IndustryDay #FederalContracting
alt_version:
If you've ever wanted to know how to work with the U.S. Coast Guard on construction and facilities projects, here's your invitation. ⚓

On March 12, SAME San Francisco Post is co-hosting an Industry Day with the U.S. Coast Guard Civil Engineering Unit Oakland. It's free, it's open to the A/E/C community, and it's built for real conversations between Coast Guard engineers and industry partners.

Here's how the afternoon breaks down:

2:00 PM — Opening Remarks
CEUO Commanding Officer and SAME SF representatives kick off the event.

2:15 PM — CEUO Briefing
Meet the branch chiefs. Learn how the unit is structured, what they build, and how the contracting process works.

2:30 PM — Panel Discussion
Upcoming contracting opportunities, challenges facing the USCG civil engineering community, and ways to improve the contracting process. Q&A included.

3:15 PM — Networking + Breakout Sessions
🤝 Conference Room H (N Tower 5th Deck): Refreshments, networking, and industry showcase with tables for CEUO reps, SAME SF, and contractors
📋 Conference Rooms A-E (N Tower 2nd Deck): Field-specific breakout sessions, followed by individual 1-on-1 meetings with CEUO contracting personnel (advance signup required — signups sent before event)

4:45 PM — Closing Remarks
5:00 PM — Event complete. No-host social to follow (location TBD) 🍻

This event is for general contractors, specialty subs, A/E firms, small businesses, and anyone in the construction and engineering space who wants direct access to Coast Guard civil engineering leadership.

📍 Civil Engineering Unit Oakland
📅 Thursday, March 12 | 2:00 - 5:00 PM
🎟️ Free

Registration link coming soon. Save March 12 now. 📌

Questions? Contact LT Duncan Clark at Duncan.G.Clark@uscg.mil

Have you worked with the Coast Guard before? What would you want to learn at an event like this? 👇

#SAMESF #AECCommunity #MilitaryEngineers #USCG #CoastGuard #IndustryDay #FederalContracting

*Note: This event does not constitute official U.S. Coast Guard endorsement of SAME or any participating organization.*
notes: Alt Opening: "Most people don't think about the Coast Guard when they think about federal construction. On March 12, we're changing that."

title: Armed Forces Day
draft:
Right now, across every time zone, American service members are standing watch. Armed Forces Day is their day. 🇺🇸

Unlike Veterans Day, which honors all who have served, and Memorial Day, which honors those who gave their lives, Armed Forces Day recognizes the men and women currently serving in uniform. The soldiers, sailors, airmen, Marines, Guardians, and Coast Guardsmen on duty today.

At SAME San Francisco Post, our mission depends on this connection between the military and civilian engineering communities. Every site visit, every Industry Day, every scholarship we fund exists because of the partnership between those who wear the uniform and those who build alongside them.

To our active-duty members and all who serve: we see the work you do, and we stand with you.

Happy Armed Forces Day. 🇺🇸

#SAMESF #ArmedForcesDay #MilitaryEngineers #SupportOurTroops #SAME
alt_version:
Every day of the year, someone in uniform is doing a job most people will never see. Standing a midnight watch. Running a convoy. Maintaining an airfield thousands of miles from home. Today, we honor them. 🇺🇸

Armed Forces Day falls on the third Saturday of May, and it carries a specific purpose: recognizing the men and women currently serving in the U.S. Armed Forces. Not those who have served before (that's Veterans Day). Not those who made the ultimate sacrifice (that's Memorial Day). Today is about the active-duty, Reserve, and Guard members on the job right now.

At SAME San Francisco Post, military-civilian collaboration isn't an idea. It's what we do. Our members include active-duty engineers from the Army Corps, Coast Guard, Navy, and Air Force, alongside the A/E/C professionals who design and build federal infrastructure beside them. That partnership shows up in our site visits, our Industry Days, and our mentorship of young professionals and STEM students.

The uniformed engineers in our network bring something our Post can't function without: firsthand knowledge of what the military needs built, maintained, and defended. The civilian engineers bring the technical depth and industry experience that turns those requirements into reality. Together, that's the SAME mission.

To every service member currently wearing the uniform, whether you're stationed at a Bay Area installation or deployed overseas: your work matters, and we're grateful for it.

Happy Armed Forces Day from SAME San Francisco Post.

How are you honoring Armed Forces Day? Tag a service member in the comments. 🇺🇸

#SAMESF #ArmedForcesDay #MilitaryEngineers #SupportOurTroops #SAME #ArmedForces
notes: Alt Opening: "They don't get a parade today. Most of them are at work. Armed Forces Day honors the people currently serving, the ones still on the clock."

title: Memorial Day
draft:
Today we remember.

Memorial Day honors the men and women who gave their lives in service to our country. Their sacrifice is the reason we can gather, build, and serve as a community.

At SAME San Francisco Post, many of our members have served alongside those who never came home. We carry that weight with us in everything we do.

No celebration today. Just gratitude, and a promise to be worthy of what they gave.

We remember.

#SAMESF #MemorialDay #NeverForget
alt_version:
Today we remember.

Memorial Day exists for one reason: to honor the American service members who gave their lives defending this country. Not a long weekend. Not a sales event. A day of remembrance for lives cut short in service to something larger than themselves.

At SAME San Francisco Post, our community includes veterans, active-duty members, and military families who carry this day differently than most. Some of our members served beside people whose names are now on headstones at national cemeteries, on walls of remembrance, on plaques at installations across the country.

We honor them not with words alone, but by continuing the mission they believed in: strengthening the connection between military service and the profession of engineering.

Their sacrifice is not forgotten.

We remember.

#SAMESF #MemorialDay #NeverForget
notes: Alt Opening: "Some gave everything. Today belongs to them."

title: U.S. Army Birthday & Flag Day
draft:
Two hundred fifty-one years. The U.S. Army is older than the nation it defends. 🇺🇸

On June 14, 1775, the Continental Congress authorized the creation of the Continental Army, a full year before the Declaration of Independence. That same date would later become Flag Day, making June 14 one of the most meaningful days on the American calendar.

For SAME San Francisco Post, the Army's birthday hits close to home. The Army Corps of Engineers has been a cornerstone of our Post since its founding. Our members work alongside Army engineers on infrastructure, flood control, environmental restoration, and construction projects across the Bay Area and beyond.

From the Revolutionary War to today's installations and civil works, the Army has always been in the business of building, not just fighting. That's a mission we understand.

Happy 251st Birthday to the United States Army. And happy Flag Day to all who honor the colors.

#SAMESF #ArmyBirthday #USArmy251 #FlagDay #MilitaryEngineers #ArmyCorpsofEngineers
alt_version:
Before there was a United States, there was an Army. 🇺🇸

On June 14, 1775, the Continental Congress created the Continental Army to fight for a nation that didn't yet exist. 251 years later, the U.S. Army remains the backbone of American defense, the largest branch, and one of the most consequential engineering organizations in the world.

That last point matters to us at SAME San Francisco Post. The U.S. Army doesn't just deploy soldiers. It deploys engineers. The Army Corps of Engineers plans, designs, and builds critical infrastructure, from levees and dams to military installations and disaster response systems. Here in the Bay Area, USACE has been central to flood risk management, navigation, ecosystem restoration, and some of the most complex civil works in the country.

Our Post exists because of the relationship between military engineers and the civilian A/E/C community. Army engineers have been part of that equation from the beginning. They sit on our boards, attend our events, lead our site visits, and mentor the next generation of engineering professionals through our Young Professionals and scholarship programs.

June 14 is also Flag Day, the anniversary of the Continental Congress adopting the Stars and Stripes in 1777. Two observances, one date, both rooted in the idea that some things are worth building and defending.

To every soldier serving today, and to every Army veteran in our SAME SF community: 251 years of service. 251 years of building what the country needs.

Happy Birthday, Army.

What's your connection to the Army or Army Corps? Share your story below.

#SAMESF #ArmyBirthday #USArmy251 #FlagDay #MilitaryEngineers #ArmyCorpsofEngineers #ArmyStrong
notes: Alt Opening: "June 14, 1775. A year before the Declaration of Independence, Congress raised an Army. 251 years later, they're still building."

title: U.S. Army Corps of Engineers Birthday
draft:
Two days after the Army's birthday, we celebrate the branch that put "engineer" in military engineering. Happy 251st Birthday to the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers. 🇺🇸

USACE was established on June 16, 1775, just two days after the Continental Army itself. From its first mission of building fortifications during the Revolution, the Corps has grown into one of the largest public engineering organizations in the world.

For SAME San Francisco Post, USACE isn't just a partner. It's at the core of why we exist. Our mission is to bring military and civilian engineers together, and no organization represents that intersection more directly than the Army Corps.

Here in the Bay Area, USACE manages flood risk along the Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta, maintains navigation channels in San Francisco Bay, leads ecosystem restoration at Hamilton Wetlands, and supports military construction at installations across the Western Pacific.

Our members have toured USACE projects, collaborated on federal construction, and built professional relationships through the Corps that span decades.

251 years of building what the nation needs. That's a record worth honoring.

#SAMESF #USACE #ArmyCorpsofEngineers #MilitaryEngineers #SAME #Essayons
alt_version:
"Essayons." Let us try. For 251 years, the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers has done far more than try. 🇺🇸

Established June 16, 1775, USACE is one of the oldest organizations in the federal government and one of the largest public engineering agencies in the world. The Corps plans, designs, builds, and operates infrastructure that millions of Americans depend on every day: locks, dams, levees, harbors, military installations, environmental restoration sites, and disaster response systems.

At SAME San Francisco Post, the Army Corps is central to our identity. SAME was founded on the principle that military and civilian engineers are stronger when they work together. USACE is where that principle becomes reality. Corps officers and civilian employees sit alongside A/E/C professionals at our events, on our committees, and at our leadership tables.

In the Bay Area, the Corps' footprint is significant:

- Flood risk management across the Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta
- Navigation and dredging operations in San Francisco Bay
- Ecosystem restoration, including Hamilton Wetlands and South San Francisco Bay
- Military construction supporting installations throughout the region and Western Pacific

Our Post has toured USACE projects, hosted Corps leaders at our forums, and connected young professionals with Army engineers who've become mentors and colleagues. Those relationships don't happen by accident. They happen because SAME builds the bridge between uniform and industry.

251 years ago, the Continental Congress created a Corps of Engineers to build fortifications for a new nation. Today, USACE manages over $80 billion in civil works and military construction annually, employs more than 37,000 people, and operates in over 130 countries.

That's not just engineering. That's a mission.

Happy 251st Birthday, USACE. Here's to the next chapter.

Have you worked on a USACE project? Tell us about it in the comments.

#SAMESF #USACE #ArmyCorpsofEngineers #MilitaryEngineers #SAME #Essayons #BuildingStrong
notes: Alt Opening: "Fortifications. Flood walls. Navigation channels. Disaster response. For 251 years, when the country needed something built, the Corps answered."

title: International Women in Engineering Day
draft:
Women make up about 16% of the engineering workforce in the United States. At SAME San Francisco Post, we think that number should be higher, and we're doing something about it.

Today is International Women in Engineering Day, a global celebration of the women who design, build, and lead in every engineering discipline.

Our Post invests in the next generation through STEM scholarships, mentorship, and our Young Professionals program. Leaders like Isabel Andaya, EIT, are helping shape what that next generation looks like: more connected, more representative, and more prepared for the challenges ahead.

But this day isn't just about the future. It's about the women already doing the work. The structural engineers reviewing designs at midnight. The project managers running federal construction. The environmental scientists restoring wetlands in the Bay Area. The military officers leading civil engineering units.

They're here, in our network, right now.

If you're a woman in engineering looking for a professional community that values your perspective, SAME SF is the place. Come to an event. Meet the people. See what this Post is about.

#SAMESF #INWED #WomenInEngineering #MilitaryEngineers #YoungProfessionals #STEM
alt_version:
Behind every piece of infrastructure you trust, there's an engineer. And increasingly, that engineer is a woman.

Today is International Women in Engineering Day, a day to recognize the women making their mark across every corner of the profession, from military installations to municipal water systems, from federal construction sites to research labs.

At SAME San Francisco Post, this day is personal. Our community includes women who serve as military engineers, lead A/E/C firms, manage multimillion-dollar construction projects, and mentor the students who'll follow them. They're not hypothetical role models. They're our members, our board leaders, and our colleagues.

We're also investing in the pipeline. Our Post awards STEM scholarships to students pursuing engineering degrees, with an intentional focus on building a profession that looks more like the communities it serves. Our Young Professionals program, led by members like Isabel Andaya, EIT, creates space for early-career engineers to build networks, attend site visits, and connect with established professionals who remember what it felt like to be new in this industry.

Here's the reality: women earn about 24% of engineering bachelor's degrees in the U.S. but hold only about 16% of engineering jobs. That gap doesn't close on its own. It closes when organizations like SAME create the events, the mentorship, and the professional relationships that help women stay and advance in engineering.

That's what we do. Not once a year. Every month. At every event. Through every connection.

If you're a woman in engineering or a young professional considering this field, SAME SF wants you at the table. Our next event is a chance to meet military and civilian engineers who are building careers and building infrastructure in the Bay Area.

To the women in our SAME SF community who show up, lead, and build: today is yours, and every other day, too.

Tag a woman in engineering who should know about SAME SF.

#SAMESF #INWED #WomenInEngineering #MilitaryEngineers #YoungProfessionals #STEM #WomenInSTEM #DiversityInEngineering
notes: Alt Opening: "She's not waiting to be invited. She's already building. International Women in Engineering Day is about seeing the women who are already here."

title: Independence Day
draft:
Before he was Commander in Chief, George Washington was a surveyor and military engineer. The nation's first major infrastructure project? A revolution.

Happy 250th Independence Day. 🇺🇸

From the fortifications at Bunker Hill to the bridges, airfields, and bases that support our armed forces today, military engineers have been building this country since day one. The Army Corps of Engineers, the Navy Seabees, the Air Force RED HORSE squadrons, the Coast Guard's civil engineering units: every branch carries that legacy forward.

At SAME San Francisco Post, we see it every day. Our members are the engineers, architects, and constructors who design and build the infrastructure that keeps this nation running. Military and civilian, working side by side. That's been the model for 250 years.

Today we celebrate the country, the service, and the builders who made it all possible.

Happy Fourth of July from SAME SF. 🇺🇸

#SAMESF #IndependenceDay #July4th #MilitaryEngineers #250Years
alt_version:
Before he was Commander in Chief, George Washington was a surveyor and military engineer. He mapped terrain, planned fortifications, and understood something fundamental: wars aren't won by soldiers alone. They're won by builders.

Happy 250th Independence Day. 🇺🇸

Two hundred and fifty years ago, a group of colonists declared independence and then had to build an entire nation from scratch. Roads, bridges, forts, harbors, supply lines. The Continental Army's engineers laid the groundwork, and we haven't stopped building since.

That legacy runs through every branch of the military. The Army Corps of Engineers has shaped our rivers, coastlines, and cities for over two centuries. The Navy Seabees built airfields under fire across the Pacific. Air Force Prime BEEF and RED HORSE squadrons keep our bases operational worldwide. The Coast Guard's civil engineering teams maintain the shore infrastructure that protects our waterways. Every branch, every generation, building what the mission demands.

At SAME San Francisco Post, our members carry that same mission forward. We are military engineers, civilian architects, A/E/C professionals, and students who believe that the connection between national defense and the built environment matters. We build together, we learn together, and we serve together.

On this 250th anniversary of American independence, we honor the engineers, soldiers, and builders who made this country possible, and the ones still at it today.

From SAME San Francisco Post: Happy Fourth of July to the builders, the planners, the constructors, and every American who serves. 🇺🇸

What does service mean to you? Drop a comment below.

#SAMESF #IndependenceDay #July4th #MilitaryEngineers #250Years #SAME
notes: Alt Opening: "250 years. The country that started with hand-drawn maps and earthen fortifications now builds infrastructure on every continent. It all started with engineers."

title: National Hire a Veteran Day
draft:
A veteran walks into a job interview with years of leadership under pressure, technical problem-solving in austere environments, and a track record of delivering results with limited resources. That's not a candidate who needs a chance. That's a candidate who brings an advantage.

Today is National Hire a Veteran Day.

In the A/E/C industry, the skills veterans carry translate directly. Project management. Logistics coordination. Safety discipline. Team leadership across complex, multi-phase operations. These aren't soft skills to develop. They're hard skills, already proven.

At SAME San Francisco Post, connecting military professionals to the private sector is core to what we do. Our events, our mentorship network, and our industry partnerships create real pathways between military service and civilian engineering careers.

If you're an employer in A/E/C: look at what veterans bring. If you're a veteran in transition: connect with us. If you're already in the industry: mentor someone who served.

This is what SAME SF is built for.

Connect with us at samesf.org or attend our next event. Veterans and employers both welcome.

#SAMESF #HireAVeteranDay #VeteranEmployment #MilitaryEngineers #AEC
alt_version:
A veteran walks into a job interview with years of leadership under pressure, technical problem-solving in austere environments, and a track record of delivering results with limited resources. That's not a candidate who needs a chance. That's a candidate who brings an advantage.

Today is National Hire a Veteran Day.

In the architecture, engineering, and construction industry, veteran talent isn't just welcome. It's a natural fit. Military engineers manage large-scale construction projects, coordinate logistics across multiple sites, and make critical decisions under time pressure and resource constraints. Combat engineers, Seabees, RED HORSE airmen, and Coast Guard civil engineering teams bring direct construction and infrastructure experience. And the leadership, discipline, and accountability that come with military service? Those show up on day one.

At SAME San Francisco Post, this connection between military service and the A/E/C profession is the reason we exist. Our networking events bring together military engineers, government agencies, and private-sector firms. Our mentorship relationships help transitioning service members understand the civilian industry. And our partnerships with organizations across the Bay Area create real, tangible hiring pathways.

But it takes action, not just awareness.

Here's how you can make today count:

- **Employers**: Post veteran-friendly roles and attend our next networking event
- **Veterans in transition**: Reach out to SAME SF. We can connect you with mentors and firms that value your experience
- **Industry professionals**: Offer to mentor a veteran entering the A/E/C field. One conversation can change a career

The military builds leaders. The A/E/C industry needs them. SAME SF connects the two.

Visit samesf.org to learn more, or join us at our next Post event. We'd like to meet you.

Who's a veteran hire that's made your team stronger? Tag them below.

#SAMESF #HireAVeteranDay #VeteranEmployment #MilitaryEngineers #AEC #VeteranCareers
notes: Alt Opening: "The best project managers we know learned their skills in places where failure wasn't an option. Today is National Hire a Veteran Day, and we're talking about why veterans belong in A/E/C."

title: U.S. Coast Guard Birthday
draft:
The oldest continuous seagoing service in the United States turns 236 today.

Happy Birthday to the U.S. Coast Guard. ⚓

Since 1790, the Coast Guard has protected our waterways, enforced maritime law, and responded to disasters along every American coastline. But there's an engineering mission most people don't see: building and maintaining the shore infrastructure, aids to navigation, and facilities that keep the Coast Guard operational.

That's where SAME San Francisco Post connects. Earlier this year, we co-hosted the USCG Industry Day with Civil Engineering Unit Oakland, bringing together Coast Guard engineers and Bay Area A/E/C professionals. The goal: stronger partnerships between the Coast Guard's civil engineering mission and the private-sector firms that support it.

From lighthouses and buoy tenders to modern facilities and waterfront structures, Coast Guard civil engineers build and maintain critical infrastructure across every coast and waterway in the country.

To the men and women of the U.S. Coast Guard, past and present: thank you for 236 years of service. Semper Paratus.

#SAMESF #CoastGuardBirthday #USCG #SemperParatus #MilitaryEngineers
alt_version:
The oldest continuous seagoing service in the United States turns 236 today.

Happy Birthday to the U.S. Coast Guard, established August 4, 1790. ⚓

Most people know the Coast Guard for search and rescue, maritime law enforcement, and disaster response. But behind every cutter, every air station, and every coastal facility, there's a civil engineering mission that keeps it all running.

Coast Guard civil engineers design, build, and maintain shore infrastructure across the country: air stations, housing, waterfront facilities, aids to navigation, and the specialized structures that support operations in some of the most demanding environments in the federal portfolio. It's a mission that requires the same technical expertise and project delivery skills found across the A/E/C industry.

At SAME San Francisco Post, we've seen that connection firsthand. In March 2026, we co-hosted the USCG Industry Day alongside Civil Engineering Unit Oakland, bringing together Coast Guard engineering leaders and Bay Area architecture, engineering, and construction firms. The event gave private-sector professionals a direct look at the Coast Guard's infrastructure priorities, project pipeline, and partnership opportunities.

That kind of event is exactly why SAME SF exists. Our mission is to build connections between military engineers and the private sector, and the Coast Guard's civil engineering program is one of the strongest examples of why those connections matter.

From the lighthouses that guided ships in the 1800s to the modern facilities that support today's operations, Coast Guard engineers have been building essential infrastructure for more than two centuries.

To every Coast Guardsman, past and present: thank you for 236 years of keeping our waters safe. Semper Paratus.

Interested in working with the Coast Guard's civil engineering program? Connect with SAME San Francisco Post to stay informed about upcoming events and industry opportunities.

#SAMESF #CoastGuardBirthday #USCG #SemperParatus #MilitaryEngineers #CEUOakland #CoastGuardEngineering
notes: Alt Opening: "When people think of the Coast Guard, they picture rescue helicopters and patrol boats. But behind every station and every facility, there are engineers keeping it all standing. Today we celebrate 236 years of that mission."

title: Patriot Day / 9/11 Remembrance
draft:
Twenty-five years ago today, 2,977 lives were lost.

We remember them. We remember the first responders who ran toward the danger. We remember the military engineers who deployed in the days, weeks, and months that followed to clear debris, restore infrastructure, and begin rebuilding.

At SAME San Francisco Post, many of our members serve and build so that communities remain safe. That work carries a deeper weight on days like today.

On this 25th anniversary, we pause. We remember. And we carry forward the commitment to serve.

Never forget. September 11, 2001.

#SAMESF #PatriotDay #NeverForget #September11
alt_version:
Twenty-five years.

On September 11, 2001, 2,977 people lost their lives. First responders, 343 firefighters among them, gave everything they had. In the hours and days that followed, military engineers and civilian volunteers mobilized to clear wreckage, restore critical infrastructure, and support a city and a nation in crisis.

The engineers at Ground Zero worked in conditions no textbook prepares you for. They assessed structural damage in real time, coordinated debris removal operations that lasted months, and helped stabilize the surrounding buildings and utilities. Across the country, military installations activated and communities came together.

Twenty-five years later, the buildings at Ground Zero stand again. The infrastructure has been rebuilt. But the loss remains.

At SAME San Francisco Post, our members are engineers, architects, and constructors who build the infrastructure that keeps communities safe. On this anniversary, that mission feels especially personal.

We remember the lives lost. We honor those who responded. And we continue to build with purpose.

September 11, 2001. Never forget.

#SAMESF #PatriotDay #NeverForget #September11
notes: Alt Opening: "2,977 lives. 343 firefighters. 25 years. Today we remember."

title: U.S. Air Force Birthday
draft:
Every Air Force base in the world runs on infrastructure. Runways, hangars, utilities, housing, fuel systems, hardened shelters. Someone has to build all of it, maintain all of it, and repair it when it takes damage.

That's Air Force civil engineering. And today we celebrate 79 years of it. ✈️

Happy 79th Birthday to the U.S. Air Force, established September 18, 1947.

Air Force civil engineers, including the Rapid Engineer Deployable Heavy Operational Repair Squadron Engineers (RED HORSE) and Prime Base Engineer Emergency Force (Prime BEEF) squadrons, deliver construction, repair, and infrastructure support in some of the most demanding environments on the planet. From forward operating bases to stateside installations, they keep the Air Force mission running.

At SAME San Francisco Post, we serve alongside military engineers from every branch. The Air Force civil engineering community is a vital part of our network and our mission.

To every Airman, past and present: happy birthday. Aim High.

#SAMESF #AirForceBirthday #USAF #MilitaryEngineers #AimHigh
alt_version:
Every Air Force base in the world runs on infrastructure. Runways, taxiways, hangars, utilities, housing, fuel distribution systems, hardened aircraft shelters. Someone has to design, build, maintain, and repair all of it, often on compressed timelines and in contested environments.

That's the work of Air Force civil engineers. And today, we celebrate 79 years of it. ✈️

Happy 79th Birthday to the U.S. Air Force, established September 18, 1947.

When the Air Force became its own branch, it inherited a critical truth from the Army Air Corps: air power depends on infrastructure. Without runways, there are no sorties. Without fuel systems, aircraft don't fly. Without housing and utilities, Airmen can't sustain operations. Civil engineers make all of it possible.

Two units stand out in that mission. RED HORSE (Rapid Engineer Deployable Heavy Operational Repair Squadron Engineers) deploys to build and repair airfield infrastructure in expeditionary environments. These are heavy construction units that can stand up a base from bare ground. Prime BEEF (Prime Base Engineer Emergency Force) squadrons handle base engineering, emergency management, and facility sustainment at installations worldwide.

Together, they represent a civil engineering capability that rivals anything in the private sector, operating under conditions most civilian engineers will never face.

At SAME San Francisco Post, our mission is to connect military engineers with the A/E/C profession. Air Force civil engineers bring deep experience in rapid construction, infrastructure resilience, and large-scale project delivery. When they transition to the private sector, they make our industry stronger. When they're still in uniform, our partnerships help them accomplish the mission.

To every Airman who has worn the civil engineering beret: happy 79th. Your work keeps the mission flying.

What's your connection to Air Force civil engineering? Share it below.

#SAMESF #AirForceBirthday #USAF #MilitaryEngineers #REDHORSE #PrimeBEEF #AimHigh #AirForceCE
notes: Alt Opening: "RED HORSE can build a runway from nothing. Prime BEEF keeps an entire base running. Behind every Air Force mission, there are civil engineers making it possible. Today we celebrate 79 years."

title: U.S. Navy Birthday
draft:
Before there was a nation, there was a Navy. On October 13, 1775, the Continental Congress authorized the first naval vessels, and 251 years of service began.

Happy 251st Birthday to the United States Navy. ⚓

From wooden frigates to nuclear carriers, from coastal defense to global operations, the Navy has protected American interests on every ocean. And within its ranks, the Seabees have carried the construction mission forward since 1942, building what the fleet needs, wherever it's needed.

At SAME San Francisco Post, that connection runs deep. Our members include naval engineers, Seabee veterans, and A/E/C professionals who design and build the facilities that keep the fleet operational. Naval Facilities Engineering Systems Command (NAVFAC) projects are part of our community's daily work.

To every Sailor past and present: 251 years of service to the nation. Fair winds and following seas.

#SAMESF #USNavy #NavyBirthday #MilitaryEngineers #Seabees #NAVFAC
alt_version:
Before there was a Constitution, before there was a flag, there was a Navy. On October 13, 1775, the Continental Congress voted to fit out two armed vessels, and the United States Navy was born.

Happy 251st Birthday to the U.S. Navy. ⚓

Two and a half centuries of service. From the wooden hulls of the Revolutionary War to the carrier strike groups operating across the globe today, the Navy has defended American waters, projected strength overseas, and maintained freedom of the seas through every era of the nation's history.

For those of us in the military engineering community, the Navy holds a special place. The Naval Construction Battalions, the Seabees, have carried the "We Build, We Fight" mission since 1942. They build airfields, repair ports, and construct the infrastructure that sustains operations in every theater. NAVFAC, the Naval Facilities Engineering Systems Command, manages billions of dollars in military construction, shore infrastructure, and environmental programs each year.

At SAME San Francisco Post, that work is personal. Our members design, engineer, and build naval facilities along the Pacific coast and beyond. Seabee veterans sit alongside civilian engineers at our events. The relationship between the Navy's construction mission and our A/E/C community is one of the strongest threads in our Post's history.

Steven "Sid" Osgood, P.E., PMP, F.SAME, our Past President, has spent his career at the intersection of military service and engineering leadership. His example reflects what our Post is built on: professionals who serve and professionals who build, working side by side.

To every Sailor serving today and every veteran who wore the uniform: 251 years of protecting the nation. Fair winds and following seas.

Who in your network serves or has served in the Navy? Tag them below.

#SAMESF #USNavy #NavyBirthday #MilitaryEngineers #Seabees #NAVFAC #NavalEngineering
notes: Alt Opening: "251 years ago, the Continental Congress made a bet: that a young nation could defend itself at sea. On October 13, 1775, two armed vessels became the foundation of the most capable Navy the world has ever known."

title: U.S. Marine Corps Birthday
draft:
Semper Fidelis. Always Faithful. For 251 years, that has been more than a motto.

Happy 251st Birthday to the United States Marine Corps. 🇺🇸

On November 10, 1775, the Second Continental Congress established two battalions of Marines at Tun Tavern in Philadelphia. Since that day, Marines have been the first to fight and the first to build what the fight requires.

Most people know the Marines for combat. Fewer know that the Corps maintains dedicated combat engineer units, including the 1st Combat Engineer Battalion, responsible for construction, demolition, and obstacle clearance in the most demanding conditions on earth.

At SAME San Francisco Post, we have a direct connection to the Marine Corps. Our annual Holiday Gala is held at the Marines' Memorial Club in San Francisco, a living tribute to Marines who served in the Pacific during World War II. And each year at the Gala, our members collect Toys for Tots donations, continuing the Marine Corps Reserve tradition that has delivered gifts to children since 1947.

To every Marine, past and present: Happy Birthday. Semper Fi.

#SAMESF #USMC #MarineCorpsBirthday #SemperFi #MilitaryEngineers #ToysForTots
alt_version:
Semper Fidelis. Always Faithful. For 251 years, those words have defined a branch of service like no other.

Happy 251st Birthday to the United States Marine Corps. 🇺🇸

On November 10, 1775, the Second Continental Congress resolved to raise two battalions of Marines at Tun Tavern in Philadelphia. What started as a small fighting force has become one of the most respected military institutions in the world, with a culture of discipline, adaptability, and mission focus that runs from recruit to general.

Within the Corps, engineers carry a tradition of their own. Marine combat engineer units, including the 1st Combat Engineer Battalion at Camp Pendleton, handle construction, demolition, route clearance, and obstacle breaching under the most difficult conditions any builder will ever face. When the mission calls for a road, a bridge, or a fortification in a combat zone, Marine engineers answer.

That engineering mission is something our SAME San Francisco Post members understand well. Military and civilian engineers in our network design, build, and maintain the installations and infrastructure that support Marine operations across the Pacific and beyond.

Our connection to the Marine Corps goes beyond the professional. Every December, our Holiday Gala fills the ballroom at the Marines' Memorial Club in San Francisco. The Club itself is a tribute to the Marines and service members who fought in the Pacific Theater during World War II. Walking through its halls, past the photos and memorabilia, is a reminder of the sacrifices that make our work meaningful.

And at each Gala, our members carry forward another Marine tradition: Toys for Tots. The Marine Corps Reserve program has been putting gifts in children's hands since 1947, and SAME SF is honored to collect donations in support of that mission year after year.

To every Marine serving today and every veteran of the Corps: 251 years of Always Faithful. Happy Birthday.

Know a Marine? Tag them below and wish them a happy birthday.

#SAMESF #USMC #MarineCorpsBirthday #SemperFi #MilitaryEngineers #ToysForTots #MarinesMemorialClub
notes: Alt Opening: "Tun Tavern, Philadelphia. November 10, 1775. Two battalions of Marines were raised, and 251 years of 'Always Faithful' began. Today we celebrate the birthday of the United States Marine Corps."

title: Veterans Day
draft:
They designed airfields under deadline. They built bases in remote terrain. They managed construction projects spanning continents. And then they came home and kept building.

Today is Veterans Day, and we honor every American who has worn the uniform. 🇺🇸

At SAME San Francisco Post, veterans aren't an abstract concept. They are our members, our board leaders, our mentors, and our colleagues. They are the engineers, project managers, and construction professionals who carried their military training into civilian careers and strengthened our community in the process.

Our Post exists at the intersection of military and civilian engineering. That intersection is built by people, and many of the people who hold it together are veterans. They bring discipline, problem-solving under pressure, and a commitment to mission that makes our A/E/C community stronger.

To every veteran in our network and beyond: thank you. Your service matters, and so does the work you continue to do.

If you're a veteran in the engineering and construction field, SAME SF is your community. Connect with us and meet the professionals who share your background and your mission.

#SAMESF #VeteransDay #MilitaryEngineers #VeteranProfessionals #AEC
alt_version:
They designed airfields under deadline. They built bases in terrain no one else would touch. They managed construction programs spanning entire theaters of operation. And when their service ended, they brought every one of those skills home.

Today is Veterans Day, and we honor every American who has served in uniform. 🇺🇸

Veterans Day is not Memorial Day. Memorial Day honors those who gave their lives. Veterans Day honors the living, the men and women who raised their right hand, served their country, and returned to civilian life carrying experience, perspective, and purpose that few other paths provide.

At SAME San Francisco Post, we see that every day. Our membership includes veterans from every branch of service: Army, Navy, Air Force, Marine Corps, Coast Guard, and Space Force. They are project engineers at firms across the Bay Area. They are program managers overseeing federal construction. They are small business owners, senior leaders, and young professionals building the next phase of their careers.

The common thread is this: they served, and they kept serving. Not in uniform, but through the work of designing, building, and maintaining the infrastructure that supports our military and our communities.

SAME, the Society of American Military Engineers, was founded in 1920 with the purpose of connecting military and civilian engineers for national security. More than a century later, that purpose holds. And at the heart of it are veteran professionals who understand both sides of that partnership.

Our Post's events, from the Holiday Gala to our monthly programs to our scholarship fundraisers, are shaped by veterans who volunteer their time and bring their networks to the table. The engineering and construction industry is stronger because of them.

To every veteran: thank you for your service, and thank you for the work you continue to do in our community.

If you're a veteran in the A/E/C industry, or if you work alongside veterans and want to strengthen that connection, SAME SF is the place. Join us. 🇺🇸

Who's a veteran in your network? Tag them below and say thank you.

#SAMESF #VeteransDay #MilitaryEngineers #VeteranProfessionals #AEC #MilitaryToCivilian #SAME
notes: Alt Opening: "Look around any SAME SF event and you'll find them: the project manager who once led a platoon, the engineer who built forward operating bases, the contractor who learned construction in a combat zone. Today, on Veterans Day, we honor every one of them."

title: Pearl Harbor Remembrance Day
draft:
Eighty-five years ago today, 2,403 Americans were killed in the attack on Pearl Harbor.

December 7, 1941. A date which will live in infamy.

The attack changed the course of history and reshaped the U.S. military. Within months, the Navy created the Seabees to build and repair the bases the Pacific campaign would depend on. Military engineers went to work rebuilding what was destroyed and constructing what victory would require.

At SAME San Francisco Post, our connection to that legacy is direct. The military engineering mission that began in the ashes of Pearl Harbor continues in our community today.

We remember. We honor. We never forget.

#SAMESF #PearlHarborRemembranceDay #NeverForget
alt_version:
Eighty-five years ago today, the attack on Pearl Harbor killed 2,403 Americans, sank or damaged 21 ships, and destroyed 347 aircraft. In a single morning, the United States was at war.

December 7, 1941. A date which will live in infamy.

What followed was one of the largest military mobilizations in history. Among the most urgent needs: engineers who could rebuild what was destroyed and build what the Pacific campaign demanded. In March 1942, the Navy established the Naval Construction Battalions, the Seabees, in direct response to the attack. Military engineers expanded airfields, repaired harbors, and constructed forward operating bases across the Pacific Theater.

That work did not end in 1945. The military engineering mission born from Pearl Harbor carries forward in every base, every installation, and every infrastructure project our armed forces depend on today.

At SAME San Francisco Post, we serve a community of military and civilian engineers whose work traces back to that mission. Eighty-five years later, the commitment remains the same.

We remember those lost. We honor those who answered the call. We never forget.

#SAMESF #PearlHarborRemembranceDay #NeverForget #MilitaryEngineers
notes: Alt Opening: "2,403 lives lost in a single morning. Eighty-five years later, we pause to remember Pearl Harbor and the military engineering mission that rose from the wreckage."

title: National Guard Birthday
draft:
Three hundred and ninety years. Older than the Constitution. Older than the country itself.

Happy 390th Birthday to the National Guard. 🇺🇸

On December 13, 1636, the Massachusetts Bay Colony organized its militia regiments into the first units of what would become the National Guard. Nearly four centuries later, more than 440,000 Guard members serve in all 50 states, three territories, and the District of Columbia.

What sets the Guard apart is the dual mission: federal readiness and community response. When floods hit, when wildfires spread, when hurricanes make landfall, Guard engineer units are among the first to arrive. They clear roads, restore power, build temporary bridges, and keep communities connected.

At SAME San Francisco Post, we see that mission in action. Our members work alongside Guard engineers on disaster response, infrastructure resilience, and the kind of rapid construction that only the military engineering community can deliver.

Always Ready, Always There. For 390 years.

#SAMESF #NationalGuard #NationalGuardBirthday #AlwaysReadyAlwaysThere #MilitaryEngineers
alt_version:
Before there was a United States, there was the militia.

Happy 390th Birthday to the National Guard. 🇺🇸

On December 13, 1636, the Massachusetts Bay Colony organized its militia companies into the North, South, and East regiments. That decision created the oldest military component in what would become the United States. Three hundred and ninety years later, the National Guard remains the foundation of our nation's defense at home and abroad.

Today, more than 440,000 soldiers and airmen serve in the Army National Guard and Air National Guard across all 50 states, three territories, and Washington, D.C. They deploy overseas alongside active-duty forces. They respond to natural disasters and emergencies at home. And they do both with a readiness that comes from training year-round while also serving their communities as civilians.

For engineers, the Guard holds a special place. Guard engineer units are often the first to respond when disasters strike on American soil. During floods in the Midwest, wildfires in California, and hurricanes along the Gulf Coast, Guard engineers have cleared debris, repaired critical infrastructure, built emergency access routes, and restored essential services. That work is military engineering at its most immediate and its most personal.

At SAME San Francisco Post, we serve a community where military readiness and civilian infrastructure meet. The Guard's dual mission of national defense and domestic response reflects what we do every day: connecting the people who design, build, and protect the infrastructure our nation depends on.

To every National Guard soldier and airman, past and present: thank you for 390 years of service to community and country.

Always Ready, Always There.

#SAMESF #NationalGuard #NationalGuardBirthday #AlwaysReadyAlwaysThere #MilitaryEngineers #DisasterResponse
notes: Alt Opening: "Four hundred years before any other military branch existed, the militia was already standing watch. December 13, 1636. Happy 390th Birthday to the National Guard."

title: U.S. Space Force Birthday
draft:
Seven years ago, the United States stood up its newest military branch. Today, it is writing its own history.

Happy 7th Birthday to the U.S. Space Force. 🇺🇸

Established December 20, 2019, the Space Force is the youngest branch of the U.S. military and the first new branch in over 70 years. Its mission: to protect American and allied interests in space through deterrence, defense, and the delivery of space capabilities.

That mission runs on infrastructure. Launch facilities, satellite tracking stations, ground-based control centers, and communication arrays all require engineering, construction, and maintenance. Every launch pad is a construction project. Every tracking station is a facility that has to be designed, built, and sustained.

At SAME San Francisco Post, we see the Space Force as the next chapter of military engineering. New missions demand new infrastructure, and our community of engineers, constructors, and A/E/C professionals is part of that work.

Semper Supra. Always Above.

#SAMESF #SpaceForce #SpaceForceBirthday #SemperSupra #MilitaryEngineers
alt_version:
The newest branch of the U.S. military turns seven today. In that short time, the Space Force has already redefined what military readiness looks like.

Happy 7th Birthday to the U.S. Space Force. 🇺🇸

On December 20, 2019, the United States Space Force was established as the sixth branch of the armed forces, the first new military branch since the Air Force separated from the Army in 1947. Its creation recognized what defense leaders had long understood: space is a domain that demands dedicated focus, capability, and people.

Today, the Space Force operates satellite constellations, monitors threats in orbit, manages launch operations, and provides the space-based capabilities that every other branch depends on, from GPS to missile warning to secure communications.

Behind every one of those missions is infrastructure. Launch complexes at Vandenberg and Cape Canaveral. Satellite tracking stations spanning the globe. Ground-based radar and communication installations that have to be engineered for precision and resilience. The Space Force may look up, but its foundation is built on the ground.

That is where the military engineering community comes in. Designing and constructing facilities for space operations means working at the intersection of advanced technology and proven construction practice. It means adapting to new requirements while maintaining the reliability that every mission depends on.

At SAME San Francisco Post, we are watching this story take shape. Our members include engineers and constructors who work on the facilities that support space operations. As the Space Force grows, so does the engineering challenge, and our community is part of meeting it.

To every Guardian serving today: happy 7th Birthday. The mission is new, but the engineering tradition behind it is generations deep.

Semper Supra. Always Above.

#SAMESF #SpaceForce #SpaceForceBirthday #SemperSupra #MilitaryEngineers #AlwaysAbove
notes: Alt Opening: "Every satellite, every launch, every tracking station depends on what someone engineered and built on the ground. Happy 7th Birthday to the U.S. Space Force."
